title: Cakes and Pastries
content:
CAKE TYPES & MIXING

Sponge Cakes: No shortening; air is leavening. Whip egg whites and yolks separately with wire whisk; fold in flour and sugar quickly with spatula. Bake in un-oiled tube pan in slow oven (325-350°F) 40-60 minutes. Done when shrinks from pan and springs back when touched. Cool inverted on rack until stiff enough to handle without collapsing.

Butter Cakes: Made with butter or shortening. Three mixing methods:
- Conventional (rich cakes): Cream shortening until thick cream consistency; add sugar; beat until light and fine-grained; add well-beaten yolks; beat vigorously between alternate additions of liquid and dry ingredients; add flavoring; fold in stiffly beaten whites.
- Modified Conventional (quick cakes): Similar but add well-beaten whole eggs to creamed shortening and sugar.
- Muffin Method (quick cakes): Sift dry ingredients. Combine melted shortening, beaten egg, flavoring, liquid. Combine wet and dry; beat well 2 minutes.

PAN PREPARATION

Oil with un-salted fat (if butter, use oil that rises to surface). Apply with pastry brush or crumpled paper. Fill pans not more than ⅔ full for cakes to reach top when baked.

BAKING

Layer and loaf cakes: moderate oven (350-400°F). Fruit cakes: slow oven (325-350°F). Done when toothpick inserted comes out clean; cake shrinks from pan; firm to touch. Cool few minutes in pan (allows sweating on bottom to loosen); invert on rack until comes out of pan.

ICING

Apply after cake has cooled sufficiently that icing won't run. Usually ice lower surface of layers. Trim edges if necessary. For layer cakes: ice top of first layer, add second, ice top and sides. Small cakes may be dipped one side at a time into icing, then rolled in chopped nuts or coconut.

PASTRY

Plain Pastry: ½ cup shortening to 1½ cups flour standard. Cut shortening into flour until coarse granular product. Add ice water until dough just hangs together. Handle lightly and quickly. Chill before rolling.

Rolling: Turn onto lightly floured board. Roll only enough for one crust. Average recipe makes two crusts. Roll to ¼ inch thick; keep as circular as possible; make slightly larger than pan. Roll upper crust; fold half over; cut slits for steam escape.

Shaping: Fold pastry half over; lift into pan; unfold; press firmly against bottom, sides, and edge. For upper crust: moisten edge of lower crust with water; cover with upper; press firmly; trim; crimp with fork or fingers. Or apply as lattice of strips.

Baking: Pastry shells (unfilled): prick bottom and sides with fork; place smaller empty pan inside; bake hot oven (450°F) about 15 minutes until crisp and well browned.

Meringues: One-crust pies may be topped with meringue. Slow oven (325°F), about 20 minutes for tender, evenly browned product. Watery meringue = insufficient baking; tough/shriveled = too high temperature.
